era_context: The late medieval period continues with significant dynastic transitions across Eurasia. The Ming Dynasty in China is transitioning from the Hongwu Emperor (d. 1398) to his grandson, the Jianwen Emperor. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War between England and France is in a period of relative peace (truce period). The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire is expanding, and the Inca Empire is in a period of consolidation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no written record)
Americas (limited written records; Aztec and Inca records exist but are underrepresented in Western historiography)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor dies; Jianwen Emperor ascends to Ming throne
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398), founder of the Ming Dynasty, dies on June 24, 1398, at age 71. His grandson, the Jianwen Emperor (age 21), ascends to the throne. The Hongwu Emperor had designated his grandson as heir, bypassing his eldest son (who had died in 1392). This succession is controversial among the imperial princes, particularly the Prince of Yan (the Hongwu Emperor's fourth son), who will challenge the Jianwen Emperor's authority and eventually usurp the throne in 1402. The Jianwen Emperor inherits a centralized, militarized state but faces immediate challenges to his legitimacy.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Prince of Yan, r. 1402–1424, uncle)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Records, compiled contemporaneously)
Sima Guang, Zizhi Tongjian (comprehensive history, compiled 11th century but covering this period)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's death on June 24, 1398, and the Jianwen Emperor's accession are precisely documented in Ming records. This is one of the most securely dated events in Chinese history.

events.title: Jianwen Emperor begins 'Removal of the Princes' (Jingnan) campaign to reduce princely power
events.description: Shortly after his accession, the Jianwen Emperor, advised by his minister Huang Zicheng, begins a systematic campaign to reduce the power of the imperial princes. This campaign, known as the 'Jingnan' (removal of the princes), involves stripping princes of their military commands and administrative authority. The campaign provokes resistance from the Prince of Yan and other princes, destabilizing the early years of the Jianwen Emperor's reign. This policy directly contributes to the Prince of Yan's rebellion in 1402.
key_figures: Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor, primary target)
events.certainty_note: The Jianwen Emperor's 'Removal of the Princes' campaign is well-documented in Ming records. It began in 1398 and continued until 1402.

events.title: Timur prepares final military campaign against Ming China; dies before departure
events.description: Timur, the aging Turco-Mongol conqueror, begins preparations for a massive military campaign against the Ming Dynasty in China. He mobilizes a large army and plans to march eastward. However, he dies in February 1399 before the campaign can be launched. This planned invasion would have been one of the largest military expeditions of the medieval world, but it never occurs. The death of Timur marks the end of an era of Turco-Mongol conquest and the beginning of the fragmentation of his empire.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler, would have faced invasion)
events.certainty_note: Timur's preparations for a campaign against China are attested in later sources, but the exact timing and scope are debated. The campaign was planned but never executed due to Timur's death in February 1399.
cross_references: 1405-001 (Zheng He's first voyage, in part a response to Timurid threats)

events.title: Bayezid I consolidates Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402) continues to consolidate Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans. By 1398, the Ottomans control much of the Balkans and are expanding in Anatolia at the expense of the declining Anatolian Beyliks (Turkish principalities). Bayezid's military campaigns and administrative reforms are strengthening the Ottoman state. The Ottoman Empire is becoming the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans, a position it will maintain for centuries.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in 1398 is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and contemporary Byzantine sources. Specific events for 1398 are less precisely dated than major battles.

events.title: Hundred Years' War in truce period; English and French courts negotiate
events.description: The Hundred Years' War between England and France is in a period of relative peace and negotiation. The Treaty of Paris (1396) had established a 28-year truce between England and France. By 1398, both kingdoms are engaged in internal political struggles (Henry IV's rise in England, the Armagnac-Burgundian conflict in France) rather than major military campaigns against each other. This truce period allows both kingdoms to focus on internal consolidation.
events.certainty_note: The truce period of the Hundred Years' War (1396–1415) is well-documented in English and French sources.
cross_references: 1415-001 (Battle of Agincourt, resumption of major warfare)

disconfirming_evidence: The Jianwen Emperor's 'Removal of the Princes' campaign is sometimes portrayed as a rational policy to centralize power; modern scholarship suggests it was poorly executed and provoked unnecessary resistance. The campaign's effectiveness is debated—it weakened some princes but failed to neutralize the Prince of Yan, who was the most dangerous. Timur's planned invasion of China is sometimes exaggerated in scope; while it was a significant military preparation, it was not unprecedented in scale. The Hundred Years' War truce is sometimes portrayed as a period of complete peace; in reality, there were minor skirmishes and privateering, though no major campaigns.
historiographic_note: The year 1398 is very well-documented in Chinese sources (Ming Shilu provides detailed records), moderately documented in European sources, and less well-documented in Ottoman sources (which are largely compiled later). Chinese sources are official and court-centric. European sources are biased toward political and military events. Ottoman sources are retrospective and may reflect later political narratives. The absence of documentation for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas reflects the geographic bias of surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Jianwen Emperor's accession in 1398 directly led to the 'Removal of the Princes' campaign.
The 'Removal of the Princes' campaign provoked the Prince of Yan's rebellion, which culminated in the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402.
Timur's preparations for invasion in 1398 were cut short by his death in February 1399.
The Hundred Years' War truce was ongoing during Henry IV's rise to power in England in 1399.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
